lesson.menu_desc: Consolidation, Self-Assessment & Connecting Ideas with Discourse Markers — 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 10 — Checkpoint: Consolidation, Confidence Check & Building Momentum
lesson.title_tag: Professor View — Vanessa Giuriati | Aula 10 — Checkpoint

listenings.text: I want to tell you about my English journey so far. When I started, I was very shy. Honestly, I could barely say a full sentence out loud. I was always nervous, and I would freeze. However, something has changed. I am still learning, although I feel much more comfortable now. As a result, I can introduce myself, talk about my routine, share my opinions, and even make a polite request. On the other hand, I know I still struggle with speaking quickly. That said, I no longer pretend to understand. If I miss something, I simply ask. In addition to that, I have learned to soften my ideas and disagree politely. Because of that, conversations feel less scary. My goal for the next ten classes is simple: to speak with more confidence and to sound more natural. It is never too late, and I am proud of how far I have come.

Let me share a thought about learning a language as an adult. Many people feel they started too late, and they get discouraged. However, I see it differently. Adults bring something powerful to the table: patience, discipline, and a clear reason to learn. Although progress can feel slow on some days, it is real. As a result, the people who keep going always surprise themselves. On the other hand, the ones who give up never find out what they were capable of. That said, the secret is not talent. It is consistency. A little practice every day adds up. In addition to that, every small conversation is a victory. So if you have reached ten classes, be proud. Because of that effort, you are no longer a beginner. You are a learner with momentum, and momentum is everything.

extra_audio.text: First, you state your idea clearly. Then, you add a contrast with however or on the other hand. After that, you give a result with as a result. If you want to add more, you use in addition. Finally, you sum up with that said.
